title: FRED series search (BLS/FRED series-id resolution)
publisher: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis
description: The St. Louis Fed's series-search endpoint over the FRED catalog, used to RESOLVE a food/household item to its BLS/FRED Average Price (AP) series id (stage 1 of the price rail). Given an item name (e.g. 'ground beef', 'cheddar cheese') it returns candidate series; the enrichment adapter's LLM candidate-selection picks the single national/U.S. city average Average-Price series and fills the item's bls_series_id. The companion 'fred' source then reads the latest price observation for that id (stage 2). Prefer this over hand-curating series-id maps: it makes the FRED price benchmark work for any item BLS tracks.

example_asks: which BLS series tracks the national average price of ground beef
resolve the FRED series id for cheddar cheese
find the average-price series for a dozen large eggs
what series id benchmarks the U.S. city average price of whole milk

tos_note: Public U.S. government / Federal Reserve data. Free FRED API key required (register at https://fredaccount.stlouisfed.org/apikeys). Full-text search over the FRED series catalog (which mirrors BLS Average Price (AP) series); ~120 requests/min per key. Resolution quality depends on the search ranking — the adapter's candidate-selection gate only accepts an id that appeared in the results.

params.name: search_text
params.description: Full-text search over series titles, e.g. 'ground beef' or 'cheddar cheese'. Bound from the entity's label; the adapter's query-relaxation ladder progressively simplifies it when a query returns no series.

candidate_select.instruction: Pick the single record whose title is THE national/U.S. city average Average-Price series for this item. BLS prices generic commodity categories, not brands or varieties: a variety-specific item (e.g. 'Roma tomatoes', 'Russet potatoes') matches its generic category series ('Tomatoes, field grown', 'Potatoes, white'). Prefer the most specific matching series over a broad 'All ...' aggregate. Never pick a CPI/index series or a regional (non-U.S.-city-average) series
